_note: ORIGINAL content. Topic coverage follows the curriculum in Runestone Foundations of Python Programming, Think Python, and OpenStax Introduction to Python Programming. No prose, examples, or exercises were copied; the sources guide topic selection only.
title: Classes & Objects
summary: Group related data and actions into your own type.
refresher: A class describes the data and actions that its objects share. Create an object by calling the class, store its data on `self`, and use methods for work that belongs to that object. The `__init__` method sets the starting values.

blocks.body: A program may need to keep a student's name, major, and earned credits together. Separate variables become hard to manage when there are many students. A class gives that group a name and describes what each student object can do.

blocks.caption: `Student` is the class. Calling it creates one object, and `__init__` stores the values that belong to that object.

blocks.body: The name `self` refers to the object that received the method call. Python passes it automatically. `ada.add_credits(3)` uses `ada` as self and 3 as amount.

blocks.caption: A value without `self.` is only a local name. Store it on the object when another method needs it later.

blocks.title: Each object has its own data
blocks.body: Creating a second Student does not copy or replace the first one. Each call to the class creates a separate object with separate instance attributes.

blocks.code:
a = Student("Ada", 12)
b = Student("Ben", 9)
b.credits += 3
print(a.credits, b.credits)
blocks.why: The two calls create different objects. Changing b affects only b, so a stays at 12 while b becomes 12.

blocks.body: Methods keep related behavior near the data it uses. A method can return a result, update the object, or both. Give it a name that says what the object is doing.

blocks.prompt: What must be the first parameter of this instance method?
blocks.why: Python passes the receiving object into the first parameter. The usual name is self, and the method uses it to reach that object's value.

blocks.title: Class checklist
items: Use a class when data and behavior belong together.
Write `self.attribute` for data stored on an object.
Remember that each class call creates a new object.
Keep a method focused on one clear action.
